aliases: Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake 红糖糍粑
Pan-fried Glutinous Rice Cake with Brown Sugar

english: Pan-fried Glutinous Rice Cake with Brown Sugar

region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Hunan); widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for brown-sugar-glazed pan-fried glutinous rice cake; closest defensible ancestor is the generic glutinous rice cake food product class (FOODON_03315671).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers rice cake as a broad category; no sub-code exists for brown-sugar-glazed glutinous rice cake specifically. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for glutinous rice cake with brown sugar glaze.

allergens: gluten (glutinous rice flour may be processed in facilities handling wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake consists of 2–3 pan-fried glutinous rice cake pieces drizzled with brown sugar syrup, weighing approximately 150 g.

nutrient_source.title: Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake 红糖糍粑 — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake 红糖糍粑 (cr_id G00010515). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Confidence 0.95 as assigned by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake (红糖糍粑) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 86 is estimated from the closest analogous food: glutinous rice (white, cooked), GI = 86 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Brown sugar glaze adds free sugars (sucrose GI ~65) but the dominant carbohydrate matrix is glutinous rice starch, which drives the overall GI. Pan-frying does not substantially lower GI relative to boiled glutinous rice. GL per serving calculated as: (86 × 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 150 g serving) / 100 / 100 = approximately 32.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (231 mg/100 g) combined with high glycaemic load promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per occasion; the sodium content of 231 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets, and the high glycaemic load may exacerbate insulin-mediated fluid retention, compounding blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycaemic index (GI 86) from glutinous rice starch with moderate carbohydrate load (25.19 g/100 g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake; the glutinous rice base has a GI of approximately 86, and a standard 150 g serving delivers a glycaemic load of ~32.5, which will cause a significant and rapid postprandial blood glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g, high glycaemic load, and pan-frying generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this food only occasionally and in small portions; the combination of moderate sodium, high glycaemic load, and AGEs from pan-frying collectively elevates cardiometabolic risk, and the dish should not be a regular dietary component.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pan-frying generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation; high glycaemic load drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; pan-frying generates AGEs that worsen synovial inflammation, and the high glycaemic load promotes production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and TNF-α.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Lava Rice Cake (红糖糍粑, Hóng Táng Cí Bā) is a popular Chinese dessert and street snack originating from Sichuan and Hunan provinces, now widely available in Singapore's hawker centres and food courts. The dish is made from glutinous rice flour shaped into thick oval or rectangular cakes, pan-fried in oil until the exterior develops a golden, slightly crispy crust, and then generously coated or filled with a warm, flowing brown sugar syrup — creating the characteristic 'lava' effect when cut open. The texture contrasts a chewy, sticky interior with a lightly crisped outer layer, and the flavour is intensely sweet with caramel-like molasses notes from the brown sugar. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre and 231 mg sodium. The glutinous rice base has a very high glycaemic index (approximately 86), making this food clinically significant for blood glucose management. Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid it due to the rapid postprandial glucose spike from the high-GI starch. The moderate sodium content (231 mg/100 g) is relevant for hypertension management. Pan-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is typically consumed as a dessert, snack, or street food item, and is culturally associated with Chinese New Year celebrations and festive occasions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010515) with confidence 0.95 as assigned by HPB. The food is an exact match in the primary regional source. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (glutinous rice, GI 86, University of Sydney GI Database) with clear documentation.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific HPB entry is not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based glutinous rice and sugar product containing no animal-derived cholesterol. GI of 86 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice (white, cooked) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for brown sugar lava rice cake exists in approved sources. The health_suitability data in the input JSON contained internally inconsistent nutrient values (e.g., sugar cited as 22 g/100 g in reasons but HPB source reports 0.21 g/100 g); the HPB per_100g values have been used as authoritative and the health context has been rewritten accordingly. The input health_suitability entries have been superseded by the health_context section in this output. Ontology: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' — no exact ontology term exists for brown-sugar-glazed pan-fried glutinous rice cake in either FoodOn or FoodEx2; the selected codes represent the closest defensible parent classes.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed g5_ontology_honesty hard gate failure by changing foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. The value 'approximate' is not in the allowed set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; 'closest_parent' is the correct value given that no exact ontology term exists for this specific food and the selected codes represent the nearest parent class in each ontology.
Updated notes field to explicitly document the ontology match type rationale.
All other sections preserved from iteration 2 as they passed validation.
